a: Stare Miasto / Pałac Raczyńskich, ul. Długa 7
hook: Poland's deepest archive — parchment charters from the 12th century in a rebuilt Baroque palace.
facts: Founded 2 September 1808 by Prince Friedrich August of Saxony as the Archiwum Ogólne Krajowe (General National Archive), making it one of the oldest state archives in Poland.
Holds roughly 10 linear kilometres of records across ~538 fonds, spanning the 12th century through World War I.
Housed since 1952-53 in the Pałac Raczyńskich (Raczyński Palace) at ul. Długa 7, rebuilt after wartime destruction.
Its reading room is open to researchers and the public (Mon 9:00-18:00, Tue-Fri 9:00-15:30), with materials ordered in advance.
About 90% of pre-1939 Warsaw archival holdings were destroyed during the 1944 Uprising and a Wehrmacht arson on 2 September 1944.

sources.note: Founding 1808, Raczyński Palace since 1952-53, ~10 km of records, address Długa 7, coordinates 52°15'00"N 21°00'30"E
Official address ul. Długa 7, 00-263 Warszawa; reading-room opening hours

w: Poland's deepest archive — ten kilometres of records reaching back to 12th-century parchment charters, founded 1808 and now in the rebuilt Raczyński Palace on Długa. You can order documents into its public reading room. A bitter shadow: the Germans torched most of Warsaw's pre-war archives in 1944. What survived, survives here.

hook: A Renaissance-Gothic bastion guarding the Old Town gate — one of the last great barbicans left in Europe.
facts: Built around 1540-1548 by the Venetian military architect Jan Baptysta of Venice to defend the Nowomiejska (New Town) Gate.
A semicircular brick-and-stone fortress with four small turrets, part of Warsaw's double ring of city walls.
Its only major military action came in 1656, when Polish forces under King John II Casimir retook Warsaw from the Swedes.
Almost wholly destroyed in 1944; rebuilt 1952-1954 using Gothic bricks salvaged from demolished buildings in Nysa and Wrocław.
Now houses a seasonal Museum of Warsaw exhibition on the Old Town's defensive walls; the gateway passage is open year-round.
legend: Long a busker and street-artist haunt, and a popular meeting point on the wall between Old and New Town.

sources.note: Built c.1548 by Jan Baptysta of Venice, 1656 Swedish action, 1952-54 rebuild, coordinates 52.250556°N 21.010000°E
Museum of Warsaw branch; address ul. Nowomiejska 15/17, defensive-walls exhibition

w: A Venetian-built bastion of 1548 guarding the Old Town's gate — a brick semicircle of four turrets in Warsaw's double ring of walls. Its one real fight came in 1656, against the Swedes. Flattened in 1944, it was rebuilt with Gothic bricks salvaged from Wrocław and Nysa. Buskers play the wall now, between Old Town and New.

hook: A wooden Greek temple folly tucked behind the Belweder, guarded by two cast-iron sphinxes.
facts: Built around 1822, probably to a design by Jakub Kubicki, as one of three garden pavilions added when Łazienki passed to Tsar Alexander I.
Originally the 'Greek Temple' (Świątynia Grecka), later the Temple of Diana, modelled on an antique peripteral temple with an Ionic colonnade and four-column portico.
Constructed entirely of wood, on a rectangular megaron plan, with two cast-iron sphinxes flanking the entrance.
Interior walls carry original polychrome of floral and fruit motifs painted by Adam Byczkowski, alluding to Diana, goddess of nature.
Stands above a pond at the foot of the Belweder, in the part of Łazienki adjoining the Belweder garden.

sources.note: Built c.1822, Kubicki, all-wood Ionic temple, sphinxes, polychrome by Byczkowski, coordinates 52.215060°N 21.035810°E
Official Łazienki Royal Museum page confirming the pavilion and its location near the Belweder

w: A little Greek temple of wood, hidden behind the Belweder in Łazienki and guarded by two cast-iron sphinxes — built around 1822 when the park passed to the Tsar. An Ionic colonnade, a portico, walls painted with flowers and fruit for Diana, goddess of nature. A folly in the truest sense: a temple to nothing, above a pond. Pure ornament.

hook: Hand-folded pierogi on the Old Town Market Square, red-and-white checks and all — the local antidote to the Zapiecek machine.
facts: Sits directly on Rynek Starego Miasta (Old Town Market Square) at no. 16, in a rebuilt Old Town tenement.
Specialises in ręcznie lepione (hand-folded) pierogi with traditional fillings, served in a rustic red-and-white-tablecloth setting.
Regularly listed among Warsaw's best pierogarnie by local guides, distinct from the dominant Zapiecek chain.
Also trades under the name Pierogarnia Warszawska.

sources.note: Official site — hand-folded pierogi, Old Town location, menu
Address ul. Rynek Starego Miasta 16, Warszawa; corroborates location for coordinates

w: Hand-folded pierogi right on the Old Town square, red-and-white checked tablecloths and all — the local antidote to the tourist pierogi mills nearby. Traditional fillings, rolled and pinched by hand, in a rebuilt tenement on the Rynek. Among the city's best, by people who live here. Sit on the square and order a dozen.

hook: A surviving postwar bar mleczny in the shadow of the Barbakan, ladling cheap pierogi and naleśniki since the Old Town rose from rubble.
facts: A traditional Polish milk bar (bar mleczny) at ul. Mostowa 27/29, just below the Barbakan on the slope to the New Town.
Dates to the postwar years when the Old Town was being rebuilt from ruins, serving affordable Polish staples.
Still operating, typically open daily roughly 9:00-17:00.
Its name and signage are documented in the Museum of Warsaw's collections as part of the city's milk-bar heritage.

sources.note: Museum of Warsaw object — Bar mleczny Pod Barbakanem, documenting the milk bar
Address ul. Mostowa 27/29, 00-260 Warszawa; corroborates location for coordinates

w: A surviving milk bar tucked below the Barbakan on the slope to the New Town, ladling cheap pierogi and naleśniki since the Old Town was rising from its rubble. Trays, formica, subsidised prices, no concession to the tourists overhead. The Museum of Warsaw keeps its signage as heritage. The People's Republic, still serving lunch.

hook: Wine bar, cellar and shop in a historic tenement on the quieter New Town square.
facts: Located in a historic tenement on Rynek Nowego Miasta (New Town Market Square) at no. 13/15.
Combines a restaurant, wine bar and wine shop, with a professional sommelier on hand.
One of the long-running wine addresses in Warsaw's Old/New Town district, distinct from the Mielżyński wine bars.

sources.note: Official site — wine bar/restaurant/shop in a historic tenement on Rynek Nowego Miasta
Address Rynek Nowego Miasta 13/15, 00-229 Warszawa; corroborates location for coordinates

w: A wine bar, cellar and shop in a historic tenement on the quiet New Town square — a sommelier on hand, bottles to drink in or carry out, away from the Old Town crush a block north. One of the older wine addresses in this corner of the city. Sit on the gentler of Warsaw's two market squares and order a glass.

hook: Poland's first proper bubble-tea lab — Taiwanese tapioca tea brought to Chmielna from London in 2012.
facts: Opened on ul. Chmielna in 2012 as the first Bubbleology tea lab in Poland, brought over from Great Britain.
The Bubbleology brand itself started in 2011 in London's Soho.
Located at ul. Chmielna 26 on Warsaw's main pedestrian shopping street, off Nowy Świat.
Serves Taiwanese-style bubble (boba) tea with tapioca pearls; still operating (open daily, late on weekends).

sources.note: First Bubbleology in Poland on Chmielna, 2012, from the UK; London Soho origin 2011
Address ul. Chmielna 26, 00-020 Warszawa; corroborates location for coordinates and current operation

w: The shop that brought boba to Poland — a London import that opened on Chmielna in 2012, the country's first proper bubble-tea lab, Taiwanese tapioca pearls and all. Off Nowy Świat on the busy pedestrian drag, it has pulled a young crowd ever since. Not exactly traditional Warsaw, but the first of its kind here. Pick a tea, get the pearls.

hook: The marble-tabled French bakery-bistro that made plac Zbawiciela the place to brunch.
facts: Opened in 2011 on plac Zbawiciela (al. Wyzwolenia 18) and became the defining Warsaw brunch institution.
A French bistro-bakery with an on-site boulangerie baking baguettes, croissants and brioche each morning.
Famous for its all-day 'Charlotte breakfast' of fresh bread, preserves, eggs and coffee at an accessible price.
Its success spawned further Charlotte locations in Warsaw and other Polish cities; the plac Zbawiciela original remains the flagship.

sources.note: Official site — Charlotte at plac Zbawiciela, French bakery-bistro
Founded 2011, brunch institution on plac Zbawiciela; corroborates location for coordinates

w: The French bakery-bistro that turned plac Zbawiciela into Warsaw's brunch capital — open since 2011, marble tables, a boulangerie baking from dawn. The all-day Charlotte breakfast of bread, jam, eggs and coffee comes cheap enough to linger over. It spawned imitators across Poland; this stays the original. Beat the crowd.

hook: The student club where Polish punk and rock cut their teeth — a 'cult temple' for 1980s outsiders.
facts: Founded in 1973 as the student club of the Warsaw University of Technology, in the Riviera dormitory at ul. Waryńskiego 12a.
In the early 1980s it held the status of a cult venue for punk-rock fans, a place condemned by parents.
Bands including Kult, Kobranocka and Daab debuted or made early appearances there.
Hosted song festivals such as 'Kij w mrowisko'; still operating as a roughly 1,000 m² concert and event club with two halls.
Stands by the Politechnika metro station near rondo Jazdy Polskiej.

sources.note: Founded 1973 as WUT student club, punk/rock history, Kult/Kobranocka/Daab debuts

w: The student club where Polish punk grew up — opened in 1973 in a Politechnika dorm, and by the early 1980s a cult den parents warned their kids about. Kult, Kobranocka and Daab cut their teeth on its stage. Still going, a thousand square metres of noise by the Politechnika metro. Forty years of the underground, still loud.
